tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Robert Brünauer in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.

tei:head.tei:note: Robert Brünauer’s schedule of lessons varied from one per month (November, January, June), to four (April); Tuesday was the most regular weekday, but with many divergences.

tei:div.tei:ab: 7. X. Seb. Bachs Partita I Bdur, neu; zugleich Beethoven: Appassionata, Vortrag im besondern (mit Hinblick auf die Darstellung im Tw. 7).Sonate op. 57, Der Tonwille 7 (Vienna: Universal Edition, 1924), 3–33. — The conjunction here of the Partita No. 1 and Schubert waltz(es) is interesting in view of Schenker’s intensive study of the Partita, especially the Prelude and Allemande, in October 1924, and of unidentified Schubert waltz(es), as cases of the – linear descent to be compared with that of the first movement of Beethoven Op. 106. For more on this, see Elias’s lesson for October 10. Dazu Versuch, ihm zur Niederschrift der Linie in Schuberts Walzer zu bewegen. Versucht die Partita zu schreiben, taktweise das in der Stunde von mir geschriebene nachzuschreiben.
30. X. 1. Satz der Appassionata zuende.
23. XI. Haydn-Sinfonie Ddur mit dem Capriccio largo in Ddur vierhändig.
8. XII. Partita vollendet.

13. XII. Brahms Gesang der Parzen, vierhändig u. Rinaldo. Macht aus Eigenem die Urlinie zu Mozart Sonate Fdur, 1. Satz (s. Cube).Most likely K 533. — Cf. Felix-Eberhard von Cube’s lessons for April 14 and 24, May 8 and 12, June 5, 19, and 23, 1925 (evidence that these lesson notes were written up toward or after the end of teaching year).
15. I. Spielt Brahms Capriccio Hmoll, neu. Liest Haydn-Sonate Gdur, Peters I, 10; beginnt das Andante der Appassionata. Reist ab, zurück
27. I. Chopin: Etude Gesdur, versucht zu schreiben u. zu spielen

10. II.
Mendelssohn: Meerestille u. glückliche Fahrt, vierhändig. Versuch, Brahms Capriccio wiederzuschreiben.
17. II. Tadle ihn wegen Geldschmutzes in Angelegenheit des Tw., es kommt zu keiner Arbeit.
27. II. Schreibt Brahms Capriccio, 2. Teil: IV–V–I. Spielt Haydn Sonate Cmoll 4/4.
3. III. Chopin Etude Esmoll; an Brahms Capriccio u den beiden Etuden.

10. III. Haydn-Sinfonie Bdur, vierhändig; liest Mozart Sonate Cdur, 1. u. 2. Satz (den ich auch Frl. Elias u. Frl. Kahn vorgelegt hatte - er liest am besten!).Cf. chronologically: Kahn: February 21 (“astonishingly bad”); Elias: February 25 (“better than Miss Kahn”). What precisely was played and compared is unclear: for Kahn and Elias it was evidently (“die”) the entire sonata; for Brünauer we know it was the first two movements; but in the parenthesis that follows, the word “which” (“den”) is singular.
17. III. Erster annehmbarer schriftlichen Beleg!Presumably evidence that Brünauer had purchased one or more issues of Der Tonwille.
2. IV. Um die mittlere Stelle in der Esmoll-Etude; Haydn-Sonate Esdur, Peters I, 3. Reist wieder ab. Fortsetzung des Haydn Esdur-Sonate.
16. IV. Haydn-Sonate Gmoll, Peters I, 4.
21. IV. Haydn-Sonate Cdur, Peters I, 5.

23. IV. Weber „Aufforderung zum Tanz,“ auch mit Urlinie. Brahms-
Gluck „Gavotte.“The Gavotte from Iphigénie en Aulide by Willibald Gluck, transcribed by Brahms for Clara Schumann.
5. V. Brahms Zigeunerlieder, Begleitung.

9. VI. Wünscht gelobt zu werden, auch wenn er nur nachschreibt, bracht Lob an sich.Schenker’s diary for June 25, 1925 records: “Brünauer schließt die Saison, vergißt die Bonbons „… ich habe gar nicht gewußt, daß Sie schon reisen.“” (“Brünauer closes the season, forgets the candies "... I was not at all aware that you were traveling so soon."”). — This suggests that Brünauer had a lesson on this date (the final lesson of Schenker’s saison) unrecorded in the lessonbook, and perhaps another around June 11.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Robert Brünauer in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: October 7: J. S. Bach Partita I B major, new; at the same time, Beethoven Appassionata, in particular performance (with reference to the presentation in Der Tonwille 7). In that connection, an attempt to motivate him to write down the line in Schubert’s waltz(es). He attempts to write down the Partita, to copy bar-by-bar the one written by me during the lesson.
October 30: First movement of the Appassionata through to the end.
November 23: Haydn Symphony in D major with the Capriccio largo in D major, played four-hands.
December 8: Partita completed.
December 13: Brahms Song of the Fates, four-hands, and Rinaldo. On his own i.e. at home, writes down the Urlinie for the first movement of the Mozart Sonata in F major (cf. Cube).
January 15: Plays Brahms Capriccio in B minor, new. Reads Haydn Sonata in G major, Peters I, 10; begins the Andante of the Appassionata. Travels; back on:
January 27: Chopin: Etude in G major, attempts to write the line and to play.

February 10:
Mendelssohn Overture Calm Sea and Prosperous Voyage, played four-hands. Second attempt to write down the line of Brahms Capriccio.
February 17: I reproach him with miserliness regarding Der Tonwille, it doesn’t take any effort.
February 27: Writes out the line of Brahms Capriccio, section 2: IV–V–I. Plays Haydn Sonata in C minor 4/4.
March 3: Chopin Etude in E minor; on to Brahms Capriccio and the two Etudes.
March 10: Haydn Symphony in B major, four-hands; he reads the Mozart Sonata in C major, first and second movements (which movement I had set before Miss Elias and Miss Kahn – he reads best!).
March 17: First acceptable written receipt!
April 2: About the central passage of the E minor Etude; Haydn Sonata in E major, Peters vol. I, No. 3. Travels again. Continuation of the Haydn E major Sonata.
April 16: Haydn Sonata in G minor, Peters vol. I, No. 4.
April 21: Haydn Sonata in C major, Peters vol. I, No. 5.

April 23: Weber, Invitation to the dance also with Urlinie. Brahms
Gluck Gavotte.
May 5: Brahms Gypsy Songs, accompaniment.
June 9: He wishes to be praised, even when he only copies; he bestows praise on himself.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for von Cube in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: 3. X. Generalbaß fortgesetzt vom VIII Kap, 2. Abschnitt.C. P. E. Bach, Essay on the True Art of Playing Keyboard Instruments, Eng. transl. William J. Mitchell (New York: W. W. Norton; London: Cassell, 1949; London: Eulenburg, 1974), chap. VIII (II), pp. 248–52, “The Six-Five Chord (II).” — In 1924/25, Cube took lessons with Schenker four times a month. In October to December these were on Wednesdays and Saturdays; from March to June on Mondays and Thursdays. There were occasional substitutions and extra lessons. Durchsicht der Sommerarbeit, der Schubert-Walzer 1–5; Tänze u. eine Ouvertüre neu. Fortsetzung der Händel-Variationen von der 9. an.

14. X. Variationenthema in Emoll, Var. 1–2 neu u. überraschend gut.Clearly the beginning of a variation set by Cube himself. Other compositional efforts occur in subsequent lessons. — Schenker’s diary for October 14 reports: “Cube brings two nice variations on his theme; I play him a few passages from some Beethoven variations that he does not know.”
17. X. 3. Var. weniger gut.

21. X. Ueber den Tonraum gemäß den “Erläuterungen.”Reference is to Schenker’s article Erläuterungen, Der Tonwille 8/9 (Vienna: Universal Edition, April/Sept 1924), 49–51, in which he discusses the concept of “Tonraum” and related terms.
31. X. Eine andere 3. Var.A replacement for that of October 17. — Schenker’s diary for October 31 reports only: “I send Cube away quickly (Friday).”

3. XI. Korrektur der Urlinie zur Chopin-Etude Esmoll, Mittelteil (im Sommer eingesandt).Later Schenker published: Chopin: Etude Es-Moll, op. 10, Nr. 6, Das Meisterwerk in der Musik, vol. I (Munich: Drei Masken Verlag, 1925), pp. 145–59.
7. XI. Chopin Etude zuende das Blatt zurückgegeben. Zeige die autographen Blätter zu Bach.
14. XI. Bemerkungen zur Variation in Edur.

22. XI. substitute for Nov 21 Vorschläge zum Variationenwerk: über eine Variation in Cis u. Amoll
zu Emoll zurückzukehren.Schenker’s diary for November 21 records that he canceled Cube’s and Elias’s lessons for Friday November 21 on account of painful dental work.
9. XII. Fingersatz bis zur Fuge in den Händel-Variationen.
16. XII. Fuge.

19. XII. Generalbaß bis zum XIV. Kap. Verreist.C. P. E. Bach, Essay on the True Art of Playing Keyboard Instruments, chap. XIV, pp. 283–86, “The Seven-Six Chord.” — Schenker’s diary for December 19 confirms: “Cube is away for three weeks” January 8, 1925: “will not come until a week from now”; January 16: “will not come until the end of January”; January 29: “wants to stay with his father until into February; February 23: “will not come until March”; March 12: “appears at 10.30, he brings $120, shows the picture of his bride.” Kommt am
13. III. bringt neue Walzer, Tarantella, 1. Korrektur.
17. III. Korrektur des Walzers bis zur Coda.
24. III. Neues Präludium in Cmoll, sehr gut!Schenker’s diary for March 24 reports: “Cube brings a nice piece: Prelude in C minor.”
3. IV. Sonaten-Anfang: verfehlt; zeige Scarlatti Dmoll; an Brahms Fuge.Schenker’s diary for April 3 records: “Cube brings Kronen (37,000 more Kronen).”
10. IV. Korrekturen zum Walzer; über die Sonatenform, besonders den 1. Teil; Beispiele dazu: Chopin Hmoll, Scarlatti Dmoll.

14. IV. Bringt die Urlinie des 1. Satzes von Mozarts Fdur-Sonate: im Entscheidenden zwar fehlgegangen, dennoch Einzelheiten treffend. erzähle ihm, daß sein Präludium auf Weisse u. Furtwängler keinen Eindruck gemacht habe; neue Coda zum Walzer.Schenker’s diary for two days later records: “I am badly tied up [...] by composition corrections for Cube.” — Schenker’s diary for April 11 records a long meeting with Weisse and Furtwängler; presumably he showed them Cube’s Prelude (presumably that in C minor praised by Schenker on March 24) in the course of that meeting.
The Mozart Piano Sonata in F major encountered first on April 14 is never specified from among the three sonatas in that key, but the presence of the Andante second movement identifies it as K 533. Cube’s work on this sonata is central to his studies in April–June 1925. He graphs the Urlinie of each movement in turn: mvt 1: April 14–24; mvt 2: May 8, April 19; mvt 3: May 12; he moves to ink by June 5, and examines the entire sonata in his last lesson, June 23. Schenker himself was at the same time using passages from this work to illustrate his Fortsetzung der Urlinie-Betrachtungen, Das Meisterwerk in der Musik, vol. I, pp. 190–91, 193; vol. II, pp. 11–12 (Eng. transl. I, pp. 106, 107–08; II, 1–2), in each case with a three-level graph.

17. IV. Aus Anlaß
der Coda Beleuchtung sämtlicher Codastücke in Chopins Walzern.

24. IV. Durchführung der Mozart-Sonate. Generalbaß: Akkord der großen Septime begonnen.C. P. E. Bach, Essay on the True Art of Playing Keyboard Instruments, chap. XVI (I), pp.293–97, “The Chord of the Major Seventh (I)”.
28. IV. Brahms-Variationen zuende gebracht.
5. V. Chopin op. 25 Etude Fmoll neu.Schenker’s diary for May 5 records: “Cube brings Reichmarks instead of dollars.”
8. V. Urlinie des Andante aus Mozarts Sonate.

12. V. Neu: Chopin Etude Gesdur, op. 25.Later Schenker published: Chopin: Etude Ges-Dur, op. 10, Nr. 5, Das Meisterwerk in der Musik, vol. I, pp. 161–73. Wiederholung einzelner Abschnitte aus Brahms’ Variationen. Einige Fragen zur Urlinie des letzten Satzes der Mozart-Sonate.
15. V. Bild des letzten Satzes.
25. V. Chopin op. 10 Etude Am, neu, Linie u. Fingersatz.

29. V. Entwurf eines ersten Gedankens zu einem Streichquartett, mißglückt. Zeige Bilder aus Mendelssohn-, Schumann-, Schubert-, Beethoven-Quartetten, Generalbaß bis zum Nonen-Akkord.C. P. E. Bach, Essay on the True Art of Playing Keyboard Instruments, chap. XVII (I), pp. 299–302, “The Chord of the Ninth (I)”.
2. VI. Vorführung des ganzen Brahms Variationenwerkes: in vielen Dingen ausgezeichnet.

4. VI. Generalbaß
bis zum Einklang beendet.C. P. E. Bach, Essay on the True Art of Playing Keyboard Instruments, chap. XXII, pp. 313–15, “The Unison”.
5. VI. Urlinie der Mozart-Sonate mit Tinte.
9. VI. Entwurf eines neuen Sonatensatzes bis zum 2. Ged.

12. VI. Die Technik einzelner hoher Finger inmitten von Figuren (zu Chopin Etude Fmoll, T. 4–5); Hinweis auf Em. Bachs Notierung in meiner AusgabeReference is to Schenker’s edition, Phil. Em. Bach Klavierwerke (Vienna: Universal Edition, 1903). u. auf Haydns Andante Fmoll, 32tel Variation.
16. VI. Noch einmal Brahms Variationen ganz.
19. VI. Etüden Amoll, Gesdur, Fmoll. letzten sehr schön. Andante aus Mozart in Tinte.
22. VI. Bach: englische Suite Amoll begonnen; einiges vierhändig gespielt.

23. VI. Mozart: alle Sätze. Zeige Mängel der Reger-Variationen.Later Schenker published: Ein Gegenbeispiel: Max Reger, op. 81, Variationen und Fuge über ein Thema von Joh. Seb. Bach für Klavier, Das Meisterwerk in der Musik, vol. II (Munich: Drei Masken Verlag, 1926), 171–92.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for von Cube in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: October 3: Thoroughbass continued from chapter VIII, Section 2. Review of his summer work, the Schubert Waltzes 1–5; Dances and an overture, new. Continuation of the Handel Variations from the ninth variation on.
October 14: Variation theme in E minor, variations 1–2 new and surprisingly good.
October 17: Third variation less good.
October 21: About tonal space according to “Elucidations.”
October 31: A new third variation.
November 3: Correction of the Urlinie to Chopin Etude in E minor, the middle section (sent in summer).
November 7: Chopin Etude through to the end; the sheet given back. I show him the autograph leaves to Bach.
November 14: Comments on his Variation in E major.

November 22:. substitute for Nov 21 Proposals regarding variation writing: on a variation in C and A minor
returning to E minor.
December 9: Fingering as far as the fugue in the Handel Variations.
December 16: Fugue.
December 19: Thoroughbass up to chap. XIV. He travels. appears again on:
March 13: Brings new waltzes and a Tarantella, first corrections.
March 17: Corrections to the Waltz as far as the Coda.
March 24: New Prelude in C minor, very good!
March 27: New Prelude – unsuccessful; I show him Smetana’s From my Life.
April 3: Sonata beginning: unsuccessful; I show him Scarlatti D minor; on to Brahms Fugue.

April 14: He brings the Urlinie for the first movement of the Mozart F major Sonata: in crucial respects it goes awry, nevertheless, admittedly there are felicitous details; I tell him that his Prelude has made no impression on Weisse and Furtwängler; new Coda to his Waltz.

April 17: I take advantage
of the Coda to provide illumination using a collection of coda sections in Chopin’s Waltzes.
April 24: Development section of the Mozart Sonata. Thoroughbass: chord of the major seventh begun.
April 28: Brahms Variations brought to a close.
May 5: Chopin Etude in F minor, Op. 25, new.
May 8: Urlinie of the Andante from the Mozart Sonata.
May 12: New: Chopin Etude in G major, Op. 25. Revisiting specific sections of the Brahms Variations. Several question about the Urlinie for the last movement of the Mozart Sonata.
May 15: Graph of the last movement.
May 25: Chopin Etude in A minor, Op. 10, new, line and fingering.
May 29: Sketch of the first theme for a string quartet – unsuccessful. I show him graphs of quartets by Mendelssohn, Schumann, Schubert, and Beethoven; thoroughbass as far as the Chord of the Ninth.
June 2: He performs the whole of the Brahms Variations to me: in many respects excellent.

June 4:Thoroughbass
finished as far as the “Unison.”
June 5: Urlinie of the Mozart Sonata in ink.
June 9: Sketch of a new sonata movement as far as the second theme.

June 12: The technique of individual raised fingers within figures (cf. Chopin Etude in F minor, T. 4–5); evidence from C. P. E. Bach‘s notation in my edition and from Haydn’s Andante in F minor, 32nd variation.
June 16: Once again Brahms Variations played entire.
June 19: Etudes in A minor, G major, and F minor, the last one very lovely. The Andante of the Mozart in ink.
June 22: Bach English Suite in A minor begun. Several things played four-hands.
June 23: Mozart: all movements. I demonstrate the deficiencies of the Reger Variations.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Angi Elias in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab:
undated:
op.106 begonnen. op. 10, 2Probably Beethoven’s Sonata Op. 10 No. 2, a piece Elias studied the previous year. Her most recent lesson on Chopin’s Etude Op. 10 No. 2 was during the 1917/18 season. — This first entry is undated: The most likely dates for the lesson to have taken place are October 1, 4, 6, or 8. Elias seems to have signed up for three lessons per week, on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Saturdays, but with many omissions/absences and several substitutions. wiederholt, Haydn Cismoll, letzter Satz, gelesen.

10. X. Abschluss des 1. Teiles von op. 106, dazu Theoretisches über – u. das Verhältnis von Tonalität u. Form. Vergleiche mit Bachs Partita,Partita No. 1 in B major, BWV 825, on which another pupil (Brünauer) had just received a lesson three days earlier. In a series of sketches that Schenker appears to have prepared the following week (OC 69/4–8), he graphs both the Prelude and Allemande as a - linear descent. Cf. Schenker’s diary, October 15: “An op. 106 u. dem Präludium der Partita u. Allemande.” (“[Work] on Op. 106 and the Prelude of the Partita and Allemande.”) October 17: “Allemande zuende.” (“Allemande finished.”) Präludium, Ale-
mande u. mit Schuberts Walzer

29. X.. Urlinie u. Klischées bis zum 2. Ged. in op. 106,OC 65/12, Elias’s foreground graph of the first 161 measures of this movement, resembles Schenker’s “Urlinie-Tafel” graphs in its level of detail, while OC 65/4, her two-level graph of the first 134 bars, is labelled as “I, Klischees.” This lesson appears to have centered on bars 1–61 of these two documents, comprising the first theme (“Gruppe I”) and transition (“Modulationspartie”) in Schenker’s earlier analysis of the movement’s form (OC 65/64–65). See Nicholas Marston, Heinrich Schenker and Beethoven’s ‘Hammerklavier’ Sonata, RMA Monograph 23 (Farnham, Surrey, and Burlington, VT: RMA/Ashgate, 2013), pp. 36–42 and 149–50.
Klischée: literally “printing block,” but often used by Schenker to mean the printed result, i.e. a music example to be embedded in type. However, in the special case of Elias’s material, it is applied to first- and third-movement graphs that (bearing in mind that the analytical volume of Op. 106 was never printed) Schenker may have “intended to be the sources from which Klischées would eventually be produced.” (Marston, p. 38, footnote 30. — The Reinschrifte for Op. 106, all in the hand of Angi Elias, are as follows: mvt 1: OC 65/4–6 (Klischees), 12–14; mvt 2: OC 65/24–25; mvt 3: OC 65/28 (Klischees), 29; OC 65/43–47. dazu Betrachtungen von Bülow, Riemann, Nagel.

8. XI.. Brahms Walzer:This was the first of twenty lessons centering on graphs of Brahms’s Waltzes Op. 39 that Elias received during the 1924/25 and 1925/26 seasons. A notebook containing Elias’s graphs of the entire opus, with corrections in Schenker’s hand, survives as OC 34/312. Urlinie-Bilder von No 1, 2, 3 u. Vortrag. Gelesen: Haydn, Ddur-Sonate, Peters I, 1. Satz;The score cited here (OC score 73) includes two sonatas in D major, namely Hob. XVI/19 and 37. The lessonbook entry for November 17 suggests that Elias played the former. Brahms Walzer No 4 u. 5.

17. XI.. op. 106 fortgesetzt; Haydn Ddur Sonate, Adagio.Of the two sonatas in D major found in the score cited in the lessonbook entry from November 8, only one includes an Adagio movement (“Adagio, ma non troppo”): Hob. XVI/19.
19. XI.. op. 106 Bild der Durchführung.See Elias’s foreground graph of the movement (OC 65/12–14), bars 120–226.
22. XI.. Brahms Walzer No 6.See footnote in lessonbook entry from November 8.
24. XI.. Wiederholung des 1. Satzes op. 106; Haydn-Sonate, letzter Satz.Hob. XVI/19. See lessonbook entries from November 8 and 17.
28. XI.. Weitere Bilder zu den Brahms Walzern.See footnote in lessonbook entry from November 8.
1. XII.. op. 106: Scherzo begonnen, Winke zur Urlinie. 1. Satz schon flüssig.
3. XII.. Erster Bildversuch des Scherzo.There are two graphs of this movement in Elias’s hand: OC 65/24 (foreground) and OC 65/25 (reduction).
10. XII.. Scherzo-Trio Bilder.See footnote in lessonbook entry from December 3.

12. XII. Bemerkungen zum Weihnachts-Oratorium von Bach wegen der bevorstehenden Aufführung unter Furtwängler.This performance of the Christmas Oratorio took place on December 12 at 7 p.m. in the great hall of the Musikverein, but with Paul von Klenau as the conductor since Wilhelm Furtwängler had had to pull out in view of other commitments.
15. XII. Schöpfung, beleuchte wichtige Linien in vielen Stücken. op. 106 Scherzo gespielt.

19. XII. Brahms Walzer
Ddur, Dmoll Bilder korrigiert.
22. XII. op. 106: über das Verhältnis der Tonalität u. des Vordergrunds.
29. XII. op. 106 Adagio begonnen auch das Bild.
2. I. Brahms Walzer Gdur.
7. I. Kleines Liedchen vom kleinen Neffen; Begleitung korrigiert.
9. I. Walzer Hmoll.
12. I. op. 106 Adagio, 2. Ged.
17. I. Walzer Edur, Hdur, Bilder u. Vortrag.
19. I. op. 106, Adagio bis zur Rückleitung.
21. I. . Walzer Gismoll; über den Anfang der Nocturne Hdur von Chopin, op. 9.
23. I. Betrachtung der erleichterten Fassung des Walzer Gismoll in der Tonart Amoll.
26. I. op. 106 Rückleitung des Adagio u. erste Einführung in die Ornamente der Variation.

28. I. Letzte Walzer Asdur u. Cismoll. Schenkt aus Anlaß des zuendegehenden 20. Studienjahres das Facsimile der IX. Sinfonie.Schenker’s diary for January 28, 1925 records: “Frl. Elias bringt zur Erinnerung an ihre erste Stunde vor 20 Jahren die facsimilirte Partitur der IX. Sinfonie von Beethoven.” (“In remembrance of her first lesson twenty years ago, Miss Elias brings the facsimile score of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony.”).
9. II. op. 106, 2. Teil des Adagio.

16. II. Noch einmal die 32tel Or-
namente genau betrachtet.
18. II. op. 106 Reinschrift des 1. Satzes.

20. II. Alle Brahms Walzer, nicht übel; wiederholt Mendelssohn: Gondellied Fismoll, nach dem Tw.Mendelssohn: Venetianisches Gondellied, op. 30, Nr. 6, Der Tonwille 10 (Vienna: Universal Edition: Oct 1924), 25–29.
25. II. Mozart Sonate Cdur, die Frl. Kahn gespielt hat, besser als Frl. Kahn.Elias: February 25, cf. Brünauer: March 10.
2. III. Wiederholt Mendelssohn: Gondellied, Chopin Etude op 10 Cdur.
4. III. op. 106 Reinschrift des 1. Satzes bis zur Durchführung.
6. III. Mozart Sonate Ddur (nach einer Aufführung von Dirk Schäfer).Dirk Schäfer (1873–1931), Dutch pianist and composer.
9. III. Mozart Sonate wiederholt.
11. III. op. 106 Reinschrift bis knapp vor die Reprise.
13. III. Wiederholt Mozart Sonate Ddur, 1. u. 2. Satz.
18. III. Korrektur der Durchführung von op. 106.
23. III. op. 106 Adagio zuende.

27. III. Wiederholt Chopin Etude Asdur op 10;Schenker’s diary for March 28 records: “Chopin Etüde Asdur op. 10 auf die Urlinie untersucht (Elias).” (“Chopin's Etude in A major Op. 10 analysed for the Urlinie (Elias).”). Brahms Capriccio op 119 76 Cdur.
30. III. op. 106: erste Betrachtung des Largo.

3. IV. Chopin Etude Asdur, korrigiert nach
der Handschrift; dazu Urliniewinke.Schenker’s diary for April 3 records: “Chopin-Etüde Asdur op. 10 nach der Handschrift durchgesehen u. korrigiert für Frl. Elias.” (“Chopin's Etude in A major Op. 10 read through following the manuscript and corrected for Miss Elias.”).
6. IV. op. 106 Largo zuende.

8. IV. Chopin-Sonate Bmoll: einiges über die Urlinie, gelegentlich Müllers Studien.Siegfried Fritz Müller’s lessons for April 10, 17, 20, 24, and 27, 1925 all included the B minor Sonata, though without reference to either Linie or Urlinie. Johannes-Passion, 1. Chor.
10. IV. Wiederholt: Chopin Etudes Fdur, Cdur aus op 10.

15. IV. op. 106 Adagio-Bilder bis zur Rückleitung korrigirt. Einiges zur Urlinie der Fdur-Sonate von Mozart (s. Cube);Cf. Cube’s lesson for April 14, 24, May 8, 12, 15, June 5 (“Urlinie der [Fdur] Mozart-Sonate mit Tinte.”), 19, and 23, 1925. über die Führung der obligaten Lagen u. die irrationale Deckung der Höhe in den Meisterwerken (Weisses SextettHans Weisse, Sextett for 2 violins, 2 violas, 2 cellos, Op. 7, of which composition was completed on February 14, 1925 (Schenker’s diary), and (first?) performance in Berlin on March 10 of that year. See Weisse’s lesson notes for April 14, which include terms similar to those in this entry.).
17. IV. Wiederholt: Chopin op 10 Amoll, Cmoll.
20. IV. op. 106 Fuge begonnen.
24. IV. Wiederholt Chopin op. 10 Gesdur, Edur.

27. IV. op. 106 Bilder zum Largo u. der Fuge; Korrektur der Busoni-Arbeit. Zeige Paderewsky Platten der gespielten Etüden.Busoni made piano rolls of many works, including the G major Etude, Op. 10, No. 5; Paderewski published complete editions of Op. 10 and Op. 25, and among the recordings that he made is the Etude in C minor, Op. 10, No. 12.
29. IV. op. 106 Adagio-Bilder zuende korrigiert.

11. V. op. 106 Bilder bis zum Gesdur-Zwischensatz bars 85ff gebracht. Einiges über den Vortrag Friedmann.There was a recital by Ignacy Friedman on May 10, 1925 in the large hall of the Musikverein Building, the program being Chopin, Sonata in B minor, Schumann Papillons, and compositions by Mendelssohn, Liszt, and Bach-Busoni. The Schenkers attended with Mrs. Pairamall; there is a harsh account of the performance in his diary for that day, and also an account on May 14 of his comments to Mrs. Pairamall.

15. V.
Wiederholt Etude Cismoll.
25. V. op. 106 Fuge, Esmoll-Einsatz. bars 95ff
29. V. Wiederholt: Etude Fmoll, op 10.
30. V. Wiederholt Chopin-Walzer Cismoll.
10. VI. op. 106, 3. Satz zuendegeschrieben.
12. VI. findet beim Spiel des Cismoll Walzers endlich das, was ich unter richtigem Druck verstehe (Takt 9).
15. VI. op. 106 Fuge, Zwischenspiel Asdur bars 130ff, Hmoll bars 150ff.

19. VI. Etude Esmoll op 10, recht gut. Wiederholt Asdur-Walzer; linke Hand sogar sehr gut; findet endlich die spitze Art der Begleitung.Schenker’s diary for June 19 records: “Frl. Elias bringt als Geburtstagsgeschenk den Rest der Brahms-Briefe Bd. IX – XIV.“ (“Miss Elias brings the rest of the Brahms letters, volume IX – XIV as a birthday present.“). (June 19 was Schenker's 57th birthday.)

24. VI. op. 106 Bringt die Urlinie-Blätter vom 1., 2. u. 3. Satz samt Klischees.Schenker‘s diary for June 24 records: “Frl. Elias überrascht mich mit 540 Dollar als Vorauszahlung für die nächste Saison; trägt Bd. XIII. der Brahms-Briefe 1 nach. […] Frl. Elias bringt auch von op. 106 die Tafeln zum 1. u. 3. Satz.” (“Miss Elias surprises me with 540 dollars as a prepayment for the next season; adds volume XIII of the Brahms letters. [...] Miss Elias also brings [me] the graphs from the first and third movements of Op. 106.”).
Klischée: literally “printing block,” but often used by Schenker to mean the printed result, i.e. a music example to be embedded in type. However, in the special case of Elias’s material, it is applied to first- and third-movement graphs that (bearing in mind that the analytical volume of Op. 106 was never printed) Schenker may have “intended to be the sources from which Klischées would eventually be produced.” (Nicholas Marston, Heinrich Schenker and Beethoven’s ‘Hammerklavier’ Sonata RMA Monograph 23 (Farnham, Surrey, and Burlington, VT: RMA/Ashgate, 2013), p. 38, footnote 30.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Angi Elias in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: undated: Op. 106 begun. Op. 10, No. 2 repeated. Haydn C minor, last movement, read.

October 10. Conclusion of the first part of Op. 106, and in relation to it, theoretical points on – and the relationship of tonality and form. I compare to the Bach Partita, Prelude and Allemande,
and to the Schubert Waltz.
October 29. Urlinie graph and Klischées up to the second theme in Op. 106, and in relation to it, observations by Bülow, Riemann, Nagel.
November 8. Brahms Waltzes: Urlinie graphs of Nos. 1, 2, 3 and performance. Read: Haydn Sonata in D major, Peters I, first movement; Brahms Waltzes Nos. 4 and 5.
November 17. Op. 106 continued; Haydn Sonata in D major, Adagio.
November 19. Op. 106 graph of the development section.
November 22. Brahms Waltz No. 6.
November 24. Repetition of the first movement of Op. 106; Haydn Sonata, last movement.
November 28. Further graphs of the Brahms Waltzes.
December 1. Op. 106: Scherzo begun, tips on the Urlinie. First movement already fluent.
December 3. First attempt at a graph of the Scherzo
December 10. Graphs of the Scherzo-Trio.
December 12: Observations on the Christmas Oratorio by Bach in preparation for the forthcoming performance under Furtwängler.
December 15 Creation, I cast light on important lines in several pieces. Op. 106, the Scherzo played.

December 19: Brahms Waltzes
in D major and D minor, graphs corrected.
December 22: Op. 106: über das Verhältnis der Tonalität u. des Vordergrunds.
December 29: Op. 106 Adagio begun, and also the graph.
January 2: Brahms Waltzes, Waltz in G major.
January 7: Short song by the little nephew; accompaniment corrected.
January 9: Brahms Waltzes, Waltz in B minor.
January 12: Op. 106 Adagio, second theme.
January 17: Brahms Waltzes, Waltzes in E major and B major, graphs and performance.
January 19: Op. 106, Adagio as far as the retransition.
January 21: Brahms Waltzes, Waltz in G minor; on the beginning of Chopin Nocturne in B major, Op. 9.
January 23: Observations on the simplification of Brahms Waltzes, Waltz in G minor in the key of A minor.
January 26: Op. 106 retransition of the Adagio and first introduction to the ornaments in the Variation.
January 28: The last two waltzes, in A major and C minor. On the occasion of her twentieth year of study, she presents me with the facsimile of the Ninth Symphony.
February 9: Op. 106, second section of the Adagio.

February 16: Once again on the
32nd-note ornaments considered closely.
February 18: Op. 106 clean copy of the first movement.
February 20: Brahms Waltzes complete – not bad; revisited, Mendelssohn: Gondola Song in F minor, as per Der Tonwille
February 25: Mozart Sonata in C major, which Miss Kahn has played, Miss Elias better than Miss Kahn.
March 2: Revisited: Mendelssohn Gondola Song; Chopin Etude Op. 10 in C major.
March 4: Op. 106 clean copy of the first movement as far as the development section.
March 6: Mozart Sonata in D major (after the performance by Dirk Schäfer).
March 9: Mozart Sonata revisited.
March 11: Op. 106 clean copy to just before the Recapitulation.
March 13: Revisited: Mozart Sonata in D major, first and second movements.
March 18: Correction of the development section of Op. 106.
March 23: Op. 106 Adagio through to the end.
March 27: Revisited, Chopin Etude in A major, Op 10; Brahms Capriccio Op. 119 76 in C major.
March 30: Op. 106: first consideration of the Largo.

April 3: Chopin Etude in A major, corrected in accordance with
the autograph manuscript; I give tips on the Urlinie.
April 6: op. 106 Largo through to the end.
April 8: Chopin Sonata in B minor: some points about the Urlinie, in connection with Müller’s studies. The St. John Passion, first chorus.
April 10: Revisited: Chopin Etudes in F major and C major from Op 10.

April 15: Op. 106 Adagio graph corrected as far as the retransition. Some points about the Urlinie of the F major Sonata by Mozart (see Cube); about the conduct of the obligatory registers and the irrational covering of the high register in the masterworks (Weisse's Sextet).
April 17: Revisited: Chopin Op. 10 in A minor and C minor.
April 20: Op. 106 the fugue begun.
April 24: Revisited: Chopin Op. 10 in G major E major.
April 27: Op. 106 graphs for the Largo and the fugue; correction of the Busoni work. I show Paderewski gramophone records of the etudes that he played.
April 29: Op. 106 Adagio graphs finally corrected.
May 11: Op. 106, she brings graphs as far as the G major episode bars 85ff. Some comments on the performance by Friedmann.

May 15:
Revisited Etude in C minor.
May 25: Op. 106 fugue, the E minor subject entrance bars 95ff.
May 29: Revisited: Etude in F minor, Op 10.
May 30: Revisited: Chopin Waltz in C minor.
June 10: op. 106, third movement written out to the end.
June 12: In playing the C minor Waltz, she finally comes up with what I understand as being the correct emphasis of the bass in measure 9.
June 15: op. 106 fugue, the episodes in A major bars 130ff and B minor bars 150ff.
June 19: Etude in E minor Op 10, really good. Revisited: the A major Waltz; the left hand even very good; she at last achieves the “crisp” nature of the accompaniment.
June 24: Op. 106 She brings the Urlinie sheets for the first, second, and third movements, complete with music Klischées.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Marianne Kahne in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: undated: Wiederholt Chopin: eine Serie Nocturnes.Those listed in Kahn’s lessons for 1923/24 are Nocturnes in D major, Op. 27, No. 2, E major, Op. 9, No. 2, F major, Op. 15, No.1, F minor, Op. 55, No. 1, and F major, Op. 15, No. 2; Beethoven Op. 110 appears once at the beginning of 1923/24. — Marianne Kahn took two lessons a month, always on Saturdays. Beethoven op. 110.
18. X. op. 111; Brahms Walzer; Chopin Walzer Desdur, Asdur.
8. XI. Wiederholt op. 109.Op. 109 was referred to in Kahn’s lessons for 1923/24 only once, in early October 1923. Aus Chopin op. 25: Amoll, Emoll, Gismoll, Cismoll, Gesdur.

21. XI. Wieder-
holt op. 101 – nur das Adagio gut; aus Chopin op. 25: Desdur, Hmoll, Amoll; Berceuse etwas besser.
6. XII. Beethoven op. 31, 1: nicht gut.
20. XII. Beethoven op. 57,Schenker had published a detailed analysis of Op. 57 in Der Tonwille 10 (Vienna: Universal Edition, October 1924), 3–33. Cismoll-Sonate, letzter Satz gut.
3. I. Wiederholt Chopin Nocturnes Dmoll,There is no nocturne in D minor; perhaps one of those in G minor is intended. Fdur, Desdur, Esdur.
17. I. Wiederholt op. 106, ganz; mit der Kritik komme ich nur bis zum Adagio.

7. II. Paganini-Variationen, 1. u. 2. Heft; Walzer Asdur u. Cismoll, wegen der linken Hand.Evidently Kahn had had an accident to her left hand and elbow; by her last lesson on June 20 they were better. Beethoven: Cismoll-Sonate 1. u. 2. Satz; Chopin Nocturn Esdur, Anfang, wegen der linken Hand u. des Ellbogen-legato.

21. II. Neu: Mozart-Sonate Cdur: erstaunlich schlecht.Schenker had three pupils play this sonata within three weeks: Kahn: February 21; Elias: February 25 (“better than Miss Kahn”); Brünauer: March 10 (“He reads best.”). Wiederholt Beethoven-Sonate Fdur, op. 10, 2: ebenfalls schlecht.
21. III. Brahms-Händel-Variationen. Chopin-Prelude Desdur.
4. IV. Wiederholt op. 109 u. 110.
18. IV. Wiederholt Chopin op. 10 ganz; meistens gut.

2. V. op. 25 ganz, sehr gut.These two successive lessons and those of February 7 and June 6 recall Schenker’s recommendation of Miss Kahn to Otto Erich Deutsch, which included: “She plays from memory at one sitting Brahms's Paganini Variations, Chopin's complete Etudes Op. 10 and Op. 25, Beethoven's Op. 106, etc., etc.”
16. V. Wiederholt op. 101 u. 111; besser.
6. VI. Wiederholt op. 106, ganz.
20. VI. Wiederholt Chopin Nocturnes, Walzer Cismoll, linke Hand besser. Schließt mit der Anpreisung des “Gefühls” (!!)

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Marianne Kahne in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: undated: Revisited: a series of Chopin Nocturnes. Beethoven Op. 110.
October 18: Op. 111; Brahms Waltzes; Chopin Waltzes in D major and A major.
November 8: Revisited: Op. 109. From Chopin Op. 25, etudes in A minor, E minor, G minor, C minor, and G major.

November 21: Revisited:
Op. 101 – only the Adagio good; from Chopin Op. 25, etudes in D major, B minor, A minor; Berceuse somewhat improved.
December 6: Beethoven Op. 31, No. 1: not good.
December 20: Beethoven Op. 57, C minor Sonata, the last movement good.
January 3: Revisited: Chopin Nocturnes in D minor, F major, D major, and E major.
January 17: Revisited: Op. 106 in its entirety; I get only as far as the Adagio with my critique.

February 7: Paganini Variations, Books 1 and 2; Waltzes in A major and C minor, on account of her left hand. Beethoven: C minor Sonata first and second movements; Chopin Nocturne in E major, beginning, on account of her left hand and elbow legato.
February 21: New: Mozart Sonata in C major: astonishingly bad. Revisited: Beethoven Sonata in F major, Op. 10, No. 2: likewise bad.

March 7: Revisited: the Chromatic Fantasy: too hurried; difficult study of the trills in the Adagio of
Beethoven Sonata Op. 31, No. 1.
March 21: Brahms Handel Variations. Chopin Prelude in D major.
April 4: Revisited: Op. 109 and 110.
April 18: Revisited: Chopin Op. 10 in its entirety; mostly good.
May 2: Op. 25 in its entirely, very good.
May 16: Revisited: Op. 101 and 111; improved.
June 6: Revisited: Op. 106 in its entirety.
June 20: Revisited: Chopin Nocturnes, Waltz in C minor, left hand better. She concludes with words of praise about my "sense of feeling" (!!)

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Maria Komorn in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: 10. X. Scarlatti Sonate Fdur 3/8, neu.
3. XI. Beethoven „Die Wut über den verlorenen Groschen“, wiederholt; Beethoven: Variationen Gdur WoO 70, neu.
17. XI. Variationen zuende.

15. XII. Schumann: Kinder-Scenen 1,Schenker had analyzed this piece in Der Tonwille 10 (Vienna: Universal Edition, October 1924), 34–35. — Komorn has irregularly space lessons between October 1924 and April 1925, mostly on Mondays. 2, neu.
5. I. Neu: Schumann „Glückes genug“, „ Seltsame Begebenheiten Wichtige Begebenheit “.
31. I. „Träumerei“,Schenker had analyzed this piece in Der Tonwille 10 (Vienna: Universal Edition, October 1924), 36–39. „Am Kamin“, „Steckenpferd“, „Fürchtenmachen“, neu.

16. II. Schumann: die
letzten beiden Stücke, 12, 13, neu.
2. III. Schumann von Anfang bis Ende gespielt.
20. IV. Brahms-Gluck „Gavotte“The Gavotte from Iphigénie en Aulide by Willibald Gluck, transcribed by Brahms for Clara Schumann. u. Webers „Aufforderung zum Tanz“, neu.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Maria Komorn in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: October 10: Scarlatti, Sonata in F major 3/8, new.
November 3: Beethoven Rage over a Lost Penny revisited; Beethoven: Variations in G major WoO 70, new.
November 17: Variations through to the end.
December 15: Schumann: Scenes from Childhood, No. 1 and No. 2, new
January 5: New: Schumann Happy Enough, An Important Event.
January 31: “Dreaming”, “At the Fireside”, “Knight of the Hobbyhorse”, “Frightening”, new.

February 16: Schumann: the
last two pieces, No. 12, No. 13, new
March 2: Schumann played from beginning to end.
April 20: Brahms-Gluck Gavotte and Weber’s Invitation to the Dance, new.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Fräulein Moscisker in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: undated Brahms-Händel-Variationen, bis zur VII. Variation; wiederholt: Beethoven op. 32 31 , 2, letzter Satz. Wiederholt; Chopin Etude op. 10, Fdur.Miss Mosziker arranged with Schenker on September 25 to have one lesson per week. In reality, the lessonbook shows that she took eleven dated lessons between November and June, with an increase in frequency toward the end of the saison, all except the first being on a Wednesday, plus an initial undated lesson.
27. XI. Variationen zuende.
10. XII. Fuge begonnen.

24. XII. Wiederholt: Cis-moll-Sonate, 1., 2. Satz.Schenker’s diary for December 24, 1924 records: “Frl. Mosčisker u. Frl. Elias erhalten noch ihre Stunden.” (“Miss Moszisker and Miss Elias still receive their lessons.”). — she must have had a lesson on Wednesday December 31, since Schenker’s diary records: “Frl. Mosčisker u. Frl. Elias nehmen ihre Stunden; in die 2. Stunde wird das 10. Heft des Tonwille gebracht.” (“Miss Moszisker and Miss Elias take their lessons; the tenth issue of Tonwille is brought to the second lesson.”); but none of the pieces analyzed in Tonwille 10 appear in her lesson notes for the whole season.
7. I. Fuge beendet. wiederholt: Berceuse.Schenker’s diary for Wednesday January 21, 1925 records: “Absage Frl. Mosčisker wegen Grippe.” (“Miss Moszisker cancels on account of the 'flu.”).
4. II. Gdur Impromptu, neu.

20. V. Partita abgeschlossen.Schenker’s diary for March 20 records: “An Frl. Moscisker (K.): Mittwoch unterrichte ich.” (“To Miss Moscisker (postcard): I teach on Wednesday.”); the following Wednesday, March 25, was the Feast of the Annunciation, a public holiday; customarily, Schenker did not teach on official holidays.
27. V. Wiederholt: Chrom. Fantasie.
17. VI. Wiederholt: Cismoll-Sonate, 2. u. 3. Satz, Chopin op. 25 Cismoll-Etude.

24. VI. Wieder-
holt Chopin op. 25 Cismoll, op. 10 Cismoll, Gesdur.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Fräulein Moscisker in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: undated Brahms Handel Variations, as far as variation 7; revisited: Beethoven Op. 32 31 , No. 2, last movement. Revisited; Chopin Etude Op. 10 in F major.
November 27: Variations through to the end.
December 10: Fugue begun.
December 24: Revisited: C minor Sonata, first and second movements.
January 7: Fugue finished. Revisited: Berceuse.
February 4: G major Impromptu, new.
March 11: Bach Partita No. 1 begun; in addition, repeat of Brahms Variations.
May 20: Partita concluded.
May 27: Revisited: Chromatic Fantasy.
June 10: The C minor Sonata, first and second movements once more; revisited: Brahms Intermezzo Op. 117, No. 1.
June 17: Revisited: C minor Sonata, second and third movements, Chopin Op. 25, C minor Etude.

June 24: Revisited:
Chopin Op. 25, C minor, Op. 10, C minor, G major.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Siegfried Fritz Müller in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: 8. IV. Chopin Etude-Cmoll aus op. 10.Schenker’s diary for April 5, 1925 records: “½5h erscheint Herr Müller aus St. Gallen; wir vereinbaren 3 Stunden wöchentlich.” (“Mr. Müller from St. Gallen appears at 4:30; we agree on three lessons per week.”); for April 7: “An Chopin Sonate Hmoll für Müller.” (“Chopin Sonata in B minor worked on for Müller.”); for April 8: “Müller zur 1. Stunde.” (“Müller for his first lesson”). — The lessons are mainly on Wednesdays and Fridays, with additional days varying.

10. IV. Chopin Sonate Hmoll bis zur Durchführung.Schenker’s diary for April 13 records: “Müller erlegt das Honorar, ich erstatte 300000 Kronen zurück, die mir sein Vater seinerzeit für das Zeugnis eingeschickt hatte.” (“Müller pays the fee, I pay back 300,000 Kronen that his father had sent me for the recommendation a while back.”).
15. IV. Chopin op. 25 Etude Asdur, Fmoll; op. 10 Fdur.

17. IV. Chopin Sonate Hmoll, 2. Ged., Durchführung.Schenker’s diary for April 16 records: “Bin arg behindert durch Arbeit für Müller: Chopin-Sonate Hmoll […].” (“I am badly tied up by the work for Müller: Chopin Sonata in B minor [...].”).
18. IV. Chopin op. 25 Emoll, Amoll, Gismoll.
20. IV. Chopin Hmoll-Sonate, 1. Satz zuende.

22. IV. Wiederholt Chopin-Etude op. 25 Gismoll; neu: CismollSchenker’s diary for April 22 records: “The medallion [arrives] from Rothberger in the morning via a messenger, Müller is the first to see it. — I ask Müller to tell Buxbaum that the Trio in Smetana's Quartet is to be played faster; I explain it to Müller in the score. In reply, he says: My uncle is so conceited, he does not let anyone tell him anything – like the Viennese musicians in general, who are too full of themselves and do not want to learn anything.”. — Müller was Friedrich Siegfried Buxbaum’s nephew.

24. IV. Sonate Hmoll, Scherzo, Adagio.Schenker’s diary for April 24 records: “Lie-Liechen führt Weisse u. Müller Chopin-Etüden im Gramophon (Fr. Pairamall) vor.” (“Lie-Liechen plays Chopin etudes on the gramophone (Mrs Pairamall) for Weisse and Müller.”). Pairamall’s lesson note for April 23 clarifies: “Bringt Gramophon mit Paderewski-Platter.” (“She brings gramophone with Paderewski records.”).
27. IV. Sonate zuende. op. 25 Etude Gesdur, neu.
28. IV. op. 25 Etudes Gesdur, Desdur, Amoll.
30. IV. Etude Hmoll, Cmoll. Brahms-Paganini Variationen, 1. u. 2. Heft; im Fluge.

tei:ab.tei:note: Schenker’s diary for May 2 records: “Müller zur 12. Stunde, zahlt 312 Schweizer Franken = 60 Dollar = 4,262.500 Kronen; davon ab 300000 (s. o.).” (“Müller to the twelfth lesson, he pays 312 Swiss francs = 60 dollars = 4,262,500 Kronen; 300,000 of which deducted (see above).”).

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Siegfried Fritz Müller in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: April 8: Chopin Etude in C minor from Op. 10.
April 10: Chopin Sonata in B minor as far as the development section.
April 15: Chopin Op. 25 Etude in A major and F minor; Op. 10 in F major.
April 17: Chopin Sonata in B minor, second theme, development section.
April 18: Chopin Op. 25 in E minor, A minor, G minor.
April 20: Chopin Sonata in B minor, first movement through to the end.
April 22: Revisited: Chopin Etude Op. 25 in G minor; new: C minor
April 24: Sonata in B minor, Scherzo, Adagio.
April 27: Sonata through to the end. Op. 25 Etude in G major, new.
April 28: Op. 25 Etudes: G-flat major, D-flat major, A minor.
April 30: Etude in B minor, C minor. Brahms Paganini Variations, Books 1 and 2; in a flash.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Evelina Pairamall in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: 6. XI. Schubert: Bdur Impromptu, Thema mit Variationen. Dem Hinweis aus die Urlinie begegnet sie mit einem durchaus ungläubigen „Wozu?“Schenker’s diary records that she attended for her first lesson (having been in Venice during October) on November 6; this undated entry perhaps belongs to that day. — Mrs. Pairamall had one lesson a week, on Thursday, with a few weeks either missed or unrecorded.

13. XI. Das Werk zum
erstenmal ganz gespielt.Schenker’s diary for November 13, 1925 records: “Mrs. Pairamall pays for October – perhaps she thought she could avoid paying this month and not start paying until November. My comment " ... that is for October" convinced her otherwise and she says " ... the payment for November is not quite ready yet" –; she gives Lie-Liechen three net table runners as a present. Stays in the hallway until Dr. Brünauer comes and invites him to come visit her.”
20. XI. Schubert op. 90, 4 Asdur, op. 90, 2 Esdur.
27. XI. Schubert op. 94, 5 Fmoll; op. 94, 6 Asdur u. Asdur aus op. 142, 2.
11. XII. op. 142, 4 Fmoll, neu.Schenker’s diary for December 11 records “Frau Pairamall u. Dr. Brünauer zahlen endlich.” (“Mrs. Pairamall and Dr. Brünauer finally pay.”).
18. XII. Chopin: Walzer Desdur (Minuten-Walzer), neu; Cism-Walzer wiederholt.
15. I. Chopin: Nocturne Hdur op. 9, neu.
29. I. Wiederholt Chopin-Etude Fmoll u. Gismoll op 25, Fdur aus op. 10.
5. II. Wiederholungen: Etude Cdur, Fdur aus op. 10, Emoll aus op. 25.
19. II. Wiederholt u. dennoch wie neu: Brahms op. 118, 2, Adur.
5. III. Wiederholt: Brahms Intermezzo Bmoll aus op. 117.
12. III. Wiederholt: Intermezzo Cismoll aus op. 117.
19. III. Dieses nach der Handschrift korrigiert, hierauf wiederholt No 1 Esdur.
2. IV. Brahms-Rapsodie Hmoll wiederholt.

9. IV. Neu:
Chopin Mazurka op. 33, 2 Ddur.
17. IV.April 17 was a Friday whereas Mrs. Pairamall’s lessons were otherwise all on Thursdays. Amoll.
23. IV. Bringt Gramophon mit Paderewski-Platter.
30. IV. Mit Herrn Stadler u. neuen Platten.
7. V. Wiederholt Bach: Chromatische Phantasie.

14. V. Wiederholt die Berceuse, neu Chopin Mazurka Fismoll.Mrs. Pairamall resumed work on the Mazurka in F minor, Op. 59, No. 3 at the second lesson of the next saison, on November 16. Reist an den Lido.Schenker’s diary for May 14 records: “Mrs. Pairamall is going to the Lido [i.e. Venice], will not return for four weeks; I make some comments about Friedmann's playing, but I emphasize repeatedly that my demands have nothing to do with the podium and the general public and that Friedmann is right, if not from the perspective of art, then surely from the perspective of earnings.” — Allusion is to a recital by Ignacy Friedman on May 10, 1925 in the large hall of the Musikverein Building, the program being Chopin, Sonata in B minor, Schumann Papillons, and compositions by Mendelssohn, Liszt, and Bach-Busoni. The Schenkers attended the recital with Mrs. Pairamall.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Evelina Pairamall in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
tei:div.tei:ab: November 6: Schubert: B major Impromptu, Theme with variations. She reacted to my reference to the Urlinie with a downright incredulous “What’s that for?”.

November 13: The work played in its entirety
for the first time.
November 20: Schubert Op. 90, No. 4 in A major and Op. 90, No. 2 in E major.
November 27: Schubert Op. 94, No. 5 in F minor; Op. 94, No. 6 in A major and Op. 142, No. 2 in A major.
December 11: Op. 142, No. 4 in F minor, new.
December 18: Chopin Waltz in D major (“Minute Waltz“), new; C minor Waltz revisited.
January 15: Chopin Nocturne in B major, Op. 9, new.
January 29: Revisited: Chopin Etude in F minor and G minor Op. 25, also the F major from Op. 10.
February 5: Works revisited: Etude in C major and F major from Op. 10, also the E minor from Op. 25.
February 19: Revisited, and yet as if for the first time: Brahms Op. 118, No. 2 in A major.
March 5: Revisited: Brahms Intermezzo in B minor from Op. 117.
March 12: Revisited: Intermezzo in C minor from Op. 117.
March 19: The latter corrected according to the autograph manuscript, after that, revisited: No. 1 in E major.
April 2: Brahms Rhapsody in B minor revisited.

April 9: New:
Chopin Mazurka Op. 33, No. 2 in D major.
April 17: A minor.
April 23: She brings a gramophone together with Paderewski records.
April 30: With Mr. Stadler and new records.
May 7: Revisited: Bach Chromatic Fantasy.
May 14: Revisited: the Berceuse; new: Chopin Mazurka in F minor. She is off to the Lido.

tei:div.tei:note: The lesson reports for Hans Weisse in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
The lesson reports for Hans Weisse in the original are written continuously, without paragraph breaks or separating punctuation. They have been separated here in the interests of legibility.
